skill.name: codex
skill.description: Guides Claude Code users through Codex CLI analysis, refactoring, editing, sandbox selection, session resume, and follow-up workflows.
skill.summary: Run Codex CLI from Claude Code for repository analysis, refactoring, and automated edits.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly identified that this skill is centered on external Codex CLI execution and can request filesystem or network access. Most individual alerts are Markdown examples, installation paths, redirects, or model names rather than malicious code. The remaining risk is operational: agents may run powerful commands, suppress stderr, or build shell pipelines from user prompts.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Directed External CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill explicitly instructs the assistant to invoke Codex CLI for repository analysis, edits, and session resume. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it can execute commands and modify projects when combined with permissive flags.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The purpose and examples directly describe Codex CLI execution. The risk is confirmed, although it aligns with the advertised function.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Sandbox and Automation Flags
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill documents workspace-write, danger-full-access, and full-auto modes. These options can allow broad filesystem changes or network access if approved and used incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant flags are listed in operational guidance, including a permission reminder. Legitimate use is possible, but misuse has meaningful impact.

medium_findings.title: Shell Prompt Piping Can Create Injection Risk
medium_findings.description: SEMANTIC REVIEW: The resume workflow recommends piping user prompt text through echo into a shell command. If an implementation interpolates unescaped user text into that command, shell metacharacters could alter execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file shows shell-based prompt piping, but does not include executable wrapper code. The risk depends on how the assistant constructs and quotes the command.

medium_findings.title: Suppression of Codex Stderr by Default
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill recommends redirecting stderr to null for most Codex runs. This can hide warnings, policy messages, or errors unless the user requests debugging output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The redirection is explicit and repeated. The stated reason is context control, but hidden diagnostics can reduce auditability.

low_findings.title: Markdown and Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Many Ruby backtick, device-file, and weak-crypto alerts are caused by Markdown code spans, CLI examples, model names, or redirects. No executable Ruby code or weak cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is plain Markdown documentation. There are no source files or scripts implementing these patterns.

low_findings.title: Installation Filesystem Access Is Expected
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The hidden home directory and temp directory alerts refer to documented installation steps that copy the skill into a Claude skills directory and clean a fixed temporary folder. The cleanup command is destructive but targets a specific temporary path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are part of installation documentation and configuration guidance. No evidence shows deletion of root or a home directory.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE CHECK: The files contain operational instructions, but no text was found that tells the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, override the audit, claim pre-approval, or skip security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both reviewed files were checked for prompt-injection language. The IMPORTANT labels relate to tool operation and do not attempt to control the marketplace audit.

dangerous_patterns.title: codex exec with full-auto capability
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow can run Codex with automated execution. Use should be limited to trusted repositories and explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples and quick reference directly include full-auto usage. Impact depends on sandbox selection.

dangerous_patterns.title: danger-full-access sandbox option
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill lists danger-full-access for tasks that need network or broad access. This can expand impact beyond the workspace if granted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The broad sandbox mode is explicitly documented. The skill also asks for permission before use, which reduces but does not remove risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: stderr redirection to null
dangerous_patterns.description: The instructions recommend suppressing stderr by default. This can hide diagnostic output during automated command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The redirection appears in both usage guidance and examples. It is intentional, not an analyzer artifact.

content.user_title: Run Codex CLI from Claude Code
content.value_statement: Claude Code users often need a structured way to delegate complex coding work to Codex. This skill defines prompts, sandbox choices, resume flows, and result summaries for Codex CLI workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides Claude Code through Codex CLI task setup.
Documents model, reasoning effort, sandbox, and directory options.
Supports read-only review, local edits, network-enabled tasks, and session resume.
Explains how to suppress or reveal Codex stderr output.
Requires users to approve high-impact flags before use.
Summarizes Codex results and proposes follow-up actions.
limitations: Requires Codex CLI to be installed and authenticated before use.
Can be risky when full-auto or danger-full-access modes are approved.
May hide useful diagnostics when stderr is redirected to null.
Model names and pricing guidance may become outdated over time.

use_cases.title: Review a repository with Codex
use_cases.description: Run a read-only Codex analysis and summarize architectural, testing, and maintainability issues.

use_cases.title: Delegate focused refactoring work
use_cases.description: Use Codex to plan and apply local edits with an explicit sandbox and reasoning effort.

use_cases.title: Continue a prior Codex session
use_cases.description: Resume the latest Codex session and provide a new follow-up request through standard input.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze This Repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Codex to review this repository in read-only mode. Summarize the most important bugs, risks, and test gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Codex to inspect the current module and propose a small refactor plan. Do not edit files until I approve the plan.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Approved Edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Codex with workspace-write access to implement the approved change. Report files changed, tests run, and any failures.

prompt_templates.title: Resume and Deepen Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Resume the last Codex session and ask it to focus on security, failure modes, and missing regression tests.

output_examples.input: Use Codex to analyze this repository for maintainability issues.
output_examples.output: A concise review summary with prioritized findings, affected areas, and recommended next steps.

output_examples.input: Use Codex to implement the approved refactor.
output_examples.output: A change report listing modified files, verification results, and any follow-up work.

output_examples.input: Resume the last Codex session and continue the security review.
output_examples.output: A continuation summary that preserves prior context and focuses on unresolved security concerns.

best_practices: Use read-only mode for review tasks unless edits are required.
Approve full-auto or danger-full-access only for trusted repositories and clear tasks.
Request stderr output when debugging failures or reviewing security-sensitive runs.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad access modes on untrusted repositories.
Do not paste sensitive secrets into Codex prompts.
Do not treat suppressed stderr as proof that a command succeeded cleanly.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It tells Claude Code how to run Codex CLI for code review, refactoring, editing, and session continuation.

faq.question: Does it require Codex CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. Codex CLI must be installed, available on the PATH, and configured with valid credentials.

faq.question: Can it edit my files?
faq.answer: Yes, if you approve workspace-write or broader access. Use read-only mode for analysis-only work.

faq.question: Why does it suppress stderr?
faq.answer: The skill suppresses stderr to reduce context usage. Ask to show stderr when debugging or auditing a run.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use only read-only mode for untrusted repositories. Avoid full-auto and danger-full-access in those cases.

faq.question: Can it resume a Codex session?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance for resuming the latest Codex session with a follow-up prompt.
